nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef drop-offs in the vicinity of staghorn or fire corals and rubble mounds; depth range 2-15 m

diet.notes: Primarily a macroalgae grazer; should be offered a varied vegetarian diet including dried seaweed sheets (nori), fresh vegetables, herbivore-formulated pellets and flakes, and occasional meaty supplements such as Spirulina-enriched brine shrimp and Mysis shrimp. Generously feeding algae is critical to keep this species reef-safe.

avoidWith: very aggressive tankmates that may stress this generally peaceful species

summary: Rare Tongan endemic foxface; almost entirely black with yellow pectoral fins and a vermiculated white opercular stripe. Peaceful algae grazer with venomous fin spines; generally reef-safe when well fed.
careNotes: The Tongan Black Foxface is a rare and visually striking rabbitfish endemic to the Vava'u island group in northern Tonga. The body is nearly uniformly black, set off by yellow pectoral fins, a yellow posterior margin to the dorsal, anal and caudal fins, and a vermiculated white opercular stripe that distinguishes it from the related Fiji (Bicolor) Foxface. Like all foxfaces it is generally peaceful with non-conspecific tankmates but should not be housed with other rabbitfish unless the system is very large and a bonded pair is established. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and deliver a painful sting comparable in mechanism to stonefish venom; handle only with nets or aquarium tools, never bare-handed. The species displays a mottled "fright" colouration when stressed, sleeping, or freshly introduced — this is normal and not a sign of illness. Reef safety is conditional on a well-fed specimen: hungry individuals have been known to nip at LPS and soft corals, so generous daily macroalgae offerings (nori clips, seaweed sheets) are essential. Adults are reported to live in pairs in the wild while juveniles form small schools; in captivity most aquarists keep a single specimen, though a bonded pair can succeed in a large system. The species is listed as Vulnerable (VU) on the IUCN Red List due to its tiny endemic range and collection for both the food and aquarium trades, so consider sourcing only from reputable suppliers. Water parameters and care broadly mirror the more familiar Foxface Lo (Siganus vulpinus).
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Like other rabbitfishes the species is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner cued by lunar and tidal cycles, making replication in home aquaria essentially infeasible. Wild adults are reported to occur in pairs; whether this represents true monogamous pair-bonding or transient pairing during the spawning season is not well established.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: 125 gal minimum tank, care level Moderate, peaceful temperament except with other rabbitfish, reef safe with caution, SG 1.020-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, temperature 72-78 F (22-25.6 C), dKH 8-12, max size 9 in. Collection landing page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/foxface-rabbitfish) returned minimal data; product-specific page used instead.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), max length (22.0 cm TL), trophic level 2.0 (herbivore on seaweeds), depth range (2-15 m), endemic distribution (Tonga, Vava'u), and IUCN Vulnerable status. Confirms LiveAquaria's 9 in / ~22.9 cm figure (22 cm on FishBase).

additional.name: Wikipedia - Black foxface
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, max length (22 cm), distribution (endemic to Tonga, Vava'u), depth (2-15 m), distinctive coloration (yellow pectorals, vermiculated white opercular stripe), social structure (adults in pairs, juveniles in schools), and venomous spines (compared to stonefish venom). Confirms IUCN Vulnerable status.

additional.name: Reef Builders - Black Foxface Rabbitfish
additional.notes: Hobbyist trade-introduction source. Confirmed distinguishing features vs Fiji Foxface, fright colouration behavior, rarity in the aquarium trade, and macroalgae grazing role.

fish.breedingNotes: Presumed pelagic broadcast spawner cued by lunar and tidal cycles; no captive breeding documented.
